elements.content: Polymorphism
**Polymorphism** means "many forms." In programming, it means using the same method name to do different things depending on the object.
Different classes can have methods with the same name, but each class can implement the method differently.
**Everyday example**
Think of a "start" button. On a car, it starts the engine. On a computer, it starts the system. On a microwave, it starts cooking. Same action name, different behavior!
Polymorphism works the same way in code.
Same Method, Different Behavior
Here's how different classes can have the same method name:
Both classes have a `speak` method, but each does something different. This is polymorphism!
**Why is this useful?**
You can write code that works with different objects without knowing their exact type. The code just calls the method, and each object responds in its own way.
Here's where polymorphism becomes powerful:
We loop through different animals and call `speak()` on each. Each object responds with its own version of the method!
We don't need to check the type - we just call `speak()` and it works.
What does polymorphism allow you to do?

options: Use the same method name in different classes
Create multiple objects from one class
properties.correctFeedback: Correct! Polymorphism lets different classes use the same method name.
properties.incorrectFeedback: Polymorphism is about using the same method name with different implementations.
properties.explanation: Different classes can have methods with the same name that behave differently.

elements.content: Polymorphism with Inheritance

Polymorphism works especially well with inheritance. Let's create a parent class `Animal` and two child classes `Dog` and `Cat` that inherit from it. Each child class overrides the `speak` method with its own version.
All animals inherit from `Animal`, but each child class overrides `speak()` with its own version. This is polymorphism combined with inheritance!
Here's another example. We'll create a parent class `Shape` and two child classes `Square` and `Circle` that inherit from it. Each child class has its own `area()` method.
Both shapes have an `area()` method, but each calculates area differently. The loop doesn't care what kind of shape it is - it just calls `area()`.
In the shape example, why can we call `area()` in a loop without checking the type?

options: All shapes have the same area formula
Because of polymorphism - each class has its own `area()` method
properties.correctFeedback: Correct! Each class implements its own version of `area()`.
properties.incorrectFeedback: Polymorphism lets each shape handle `area()` in its own way.
properties.explanation: Each object knows how to calculate its own area through its implementation of the method.

elements.content: **Benefits of polymorphism**

items: **Flexibility**: Same code works with different objects
**Simplicity**: No need to check object types
**Extensibility**: Easy to add new classes later

elements.content: **The key idea**
With polymorphism, you focus on **what** an object can do (its methods), not **what type** it is. As long as an object has the method you need, your code will work!
Add a `Bird` class that implements the `speak` method.

properties.hint: Use the same method name as the parent class.
properties.correctFeedback: Perfect! Bird overrides the speak method with its own version.
properties.incorrectFeedback: Override the parent's `speak` method in the child class.
properties.explanation: For polymorphism to work, use the same method name across classes.

elements.content: Keep in Mind

items: Different classes can have methods with the **same name**
You can use the same code with **different object types**
No need to check object types - just call the method
